message.text: The authenticate_user() method (called at api.py line 57) uses f-string interpolation to build SQL queries from user-supplied username and password parameters. An attacker can inject SQL via the login JSON body to bypass authentication entirely (e.g., username: "' OR '1'='1' --") or extract arbitrary data via UNION-based injection.

JWT signing secret is hardcoded as 'super_secret_key_123' (api.py line 18). Any attacker who reads the source code or decompiles the application can forge arbitrary JWT tokens with any user_id, username, and role claims, achieving full authentication bypass and privilege escalation.

JWT tokens are signed with HS256 using a weak hardcoded secret (api.py line 65). HS256 with a short, predictable secret is trivially brute-forceable. The token carries user_id, username, and role without expiration (no 'exp' claim), so forged or stolen tokens remain valid indefinitely.

API keys are hardcoded in the API_KEYS dictionary (api.py lines 19-22): 'sk-1234567890abcdef' for admin and 'pk-0987654321fedcba' for guest. These credentials are committed to source control and grant direct access to the application.

The login response at api.py line 78 includes the user's api_key in the JSON body ('api_key': user['api_key']). API keys are long-lived credentials that should never be returned in authentication responses, as they may be cached by proxies, logged by intermediaries, or exposed in browser history.

Plaintext password is written to the audit log at api.py line 71: f"User {username} logged in with password {password}". This persists credentials in the database audit_logs table, accessible to anyone with database read access or via the IDOR-vulnerable /api/logs/<user_id> endpoint.

The error handler at api.py line 85 returns the full exception message to the client: f'Authentication failed: {str(e)}'. Internal exception details (stack traces, database errors, library versions) leak implementation information that aids further attacks.
